How virtual hearings transformed India’s courts, why Maharashtra is pruning the Ladki Bahin scheme, what China’s latest missile test means for India and the Indo-Pacific, why Trump’s Hormuz transit fee plan collapsed within a day, and why the Ayodhya trust controversy is about more than money — here are today’s Premium reads.
The pandemic derailed the best of plans. But this was also the time India’s courtrooms got on to a new track, starting virtual hearings. Despite the chaotic start patchy networks, frozen screens, and mute buttons — e-courts are now a key part of India’s justice delivery system, write Amaal Sheikh and Sohini Ghosh.
The Majhi Ladki Bahin Yojana helped power the Mahayuti alliance to victory in Maharashtra by putting cash directly into the hands of millions of women voters. Two years later, with 92 lakh beneficiaries dropped from the scheme, this report examines the political calculations, fiscal pressures, and electoral risks behind the government’s decision, and why a welfare programme once seen as a masterstroke is now becoming a political liability.

Suyash Desai explains what the launch reveals about China’s rapidly evolving nuclear capabilities, the growing gap between its doctrine and deterrence posture, and what it could mean for India and the wider Indo-Pacific security landscape.
Donald Trump’s proposal to charge a 20% fee for commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz lasted barely a day before it was withdrawn. Sukalp Sharma explains why the plan triggered alarm across shipping and energy markets, the legal and practical questions it raised, and what the episode reveals about the wider contest between Washington and Tehran over who controls one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints.

The first duty of the trustees was not to manage property but to protect the faith reposed in them by millions. That faith of millions now must be honoured, not with new promises, committees or with cosmetic administrative rearrangements, but with the full force of an independent, professional and transparent investigation. Because this was never about stolen money only, but also about stolen faith, writes Yashovardhan Azad in this column.

Akash Mandal explains why South Korea’s markets have suffered far more than Taiwan’s despite both being dominated by AI stocks, and what the divergence says about investor behaviour, leverage, and the future of the AI trade.

title: Motorola Edge 70 Max launched in India
content:
Motorola on Wednesday (July 15, 2026) expanded its Edge 70 line up and launched Motorola Edge 70 Max in India. It also debuts the company’s newly launched AI assistant called Motorola Qira to offer cross-device connections among Motorola and Lenovo devices.
Motorola Edge 70 Max has a 6.8 inch quad HD+ (2K) Extreme AMOLED display, with up to 7,000 nits peak brightness and a 144 Hz refresh rate. The phone is being protected by Corning Gorilla Glass 7i and also bears IP68/IP69 ratings for dust and water resistance.
Motorola Edge 70 Max ships with a 7,100 mAh silicon-carbon battery, along with a 90W TurboPower charging. It also supports 25W Qi2 magnetic wireless charging.
Motorola Edge 70 Max runs on Snapdragon 8 Gen 5 chipset with up to 12 GB LPDDR5X RAM and 256 GB storage. It operates on Hello UX based on Android 16 out of the box.
Motorola Edge 70 Max sports a 50 MP Sony Lytia 710 sensor with optical image stabilisation, an 8 MP ultrawide and macro lens and a 32 MP front camera.
Motorola Edge 70 Max comes in 8 GB/256 GB (₹54,999) and 12 GB/256 GB (₹59,999) variants and in Pantone Aqua Gray, Dark Shadow and Ice Melt shades. Sale begins July 20, on Flipkart, Motorola, and leading retail stores across India.

title: England vs Argentina, FIFA World Cup: Reigning champion rallies to beat tough English side, enters final again
content:
Argentina's Enzo Fernandez and Lautaro Martinez struck late goals to snatch a 2-1 win over England in the World Cup semifinal on Wednesday (July 15, 2026) to send Lionel Messi's reigning champions into the weekend showpiece match against Spain.
England vs Argentina, FIFA World Cup semifinal Highlights
Just when England appeared set for victory after Anthony Gordon's second-half goal, Argentina mounted a relentless late siege and got their reward as Fernandez levelled before Martinez completed the turnaround in the 92nd minute with Messi providing the pass for the equaliser and the cross for the winner.
The result added another unforgettable chapter to one of football's fiercest rivalries, a contest rich in history, emotion and tension from the opening whistle.
For England, dreams of a first World Cup final since 1966, when they enjoyed their only triumph at the global tournament, were dashed in the closing stages, while Argentina celebrated a comeback that kept alive their quest for another world title.
With England inexplicably parked in their own end, an Argentina equaliser felt inevitable and, after waves of late pressure, Fernandez finally broke through in the 85th minute when Messi found him in space on the edge of the box to fire home from 20 metres past Jordan Pickford into the corner.
Martinez, an 81st-minute substitute, struck the winner early in added time when Alexis Mac Allister drove a shot off the post that Messi recovered. The 39-year-old talisman drove down the right to send in a brilliant ball for Martinez to head home.
"This is really emotional," Martinez said. "The first time my dad bought me a pair of boots, I always dreamed of scoring this goal. It was really tough today.
"Enzo scored a brilliant goal and I'm confident this team is continuing to show what it’s made of."
The victory carried particular significance for 39-year-old Messi, who was featuring in what is widely expected to be the final World Cup of his glittering career.
For England, the defeat was a devastating blow after they matched Argentina for much of a hard-fought contest.
The Three Lions had appeared poised for victory after Gordon broke the deadlock in the 55th minute when Nicolas Tagliafico's attempted clearance landed at the feet of Declan Rice, who sent a through ball to Morgan Rogers.
Gordon popped up at the back post to guide Rogers' cross with his instep past goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez, sparking bedlam among the England players and fans.
But Thomas Tuchel's side failed to withstand a relentless late assault as Argentina turned the game on its head.
"Just gutted for the boys, gutted for everyone, the team, the staff, the fans," England captain Harry Kane said.
"We played a good game for the large majority of it. Once we went 1-0 up, we seemed to just try and hold on. At this level, it's not enough.
"Gutted because we've worked so hard to be here and the lads have given every last bit of running, sweat, blood, tears, whatever it is. So to fall short like we did today ... just gutted."
final between the two footballing giants needed little added drama, arriving steeped in history and expectation.
One of football's fiercest rivalries, shaped by iconic World Cup clashes and political undertones, has produced no shortage of memorable moments over the decades.
This latest chapter was no different.
Both teams navigated difficult routes to the last four, relying on resilience, composure and a knack for delivering when it mattered most.
Argentina produced more of the same on Wednesday, as a side that had repeatedly found a way to win when the odds seemed stacked against them once again leaned on late-game heroics to keep their World Cup dream alive.
Argentina's supporters vastly outnumbered England's, turning the arena into a sea of sky blue and white that made Atlanta's Mercedes-Benz Stadium feel more like La Bombonera in Buenos Aires, with their fans drowning out England's attempted pre-match rendition of "Sweet Caroline" with a wall of whistles.
off with his trademark cry of "Let's get ready to rumble!"
The players appeared to take him at his word.
Tempers flared almost immediately and it took only a matter of minutes for the simmering rivalry to spill over into a series of heated exchanges in a first half that was more notable for robust challenges than genuine scoring opportunities.
The simmering tension produced goals in the second half but after England took the lead they never really threatened again. Argentina were left to lay siege to their opponents goal with Fernandez and Martinez striking the blows that led to the final.

title: FIFA World Cup: Argentine players brandish political Falklands flag after England match
content:
Argentina players held up a political banner declaring "Las Malvinas Son Argentinas" ("The Falklands are Argentine") after their 2-1 World Cup semi-final victory over England on Wednesday (July 15, 2026), in apparent contravention of FIFA rules.
FIFA's Stadium Code of Conduct bans "banners, flags, flyers, apparel and other paraphernalia that are of a political, offensive, and/or discriminatory nature" inside stadiums.
World football's ruling body did not immediately reply to a request for comment. The question of sovereignty over the islands in the South Atlantic known to the British as the Falklands and the Argentines as the Malvinas has been a long-running sore in relations between the countries.
They fought a short conflict over the islands in 1982, in which 649 Argentine soldiers and 255 British combatants died. Britain ultimately won and the vast majority of residents of the islands have said they wish to remain part of Britain.
But Argentina has long claimed it inherited the islands from Spain after its independence in 1816 and that Britain took control in 1833 through an illegal colonial act.
Lisandro Martinez and Giovani Lo Celso held up the banner, grinning, and waved to fans in the stands. It was unclear where the banner had come from. It is not the first time the question of political banners has come up during the World Cup.
Last month in Los Angeles, Iranian Americans waved pre-revolutionary flags that are symbols of protest against the Tehran government when Iran played. Those matches proceeded without incident.

title: England vs Argentina, FIFA World Cup semifinal Highlights: Two late goals in testy contest take Argentina into another final
content:
Defending champion Argentina rallied to beat England 2-1 on Wednesday (July 15, 2026) and reach the FIFA World Cup final, four years after winning it.
Trailing 1-0 going into the last five minutes of regulation time, Enzo Fernandez and substitute Lautaro Martinez scored for Argentina. Lionel Messi assisted both goals.
Anthony Gordon had given England the lead, but Messi and Co. had other ideas in an extremely physical contest that had 26 fouls between the teams.
Argentina will play Spain in the final on Sunday.
(Commentary by Srinivasan Ramani and Vighnesh Venkitesh. Inputs from Reuben Joe Joseph)

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 | How the Spanish midfield choked France
content:
France had arrived in Dallas carrying the tournament’s most feared attack. Kylian Mbappe, Ousmane Dembele and Michael Olise had combined for 13 goals and 10 assists in six matches as opponents struggled to contain them. By the end of the semifinal, Spain had made that frightening front three look almost anonymous.
The scoreline read 2-0, but the performance was even more emphatic.
Spain reached its first World Cup final since lifting the trophy in Johannesburg 16 years ago by producing perhaps the tournament’s most complete display, suffocating Didier Deschamps’ side through relentless pressing, immaculate possession and defensive organisation.
France managed only three shots on target and rarely looked capable of disturbing a Spanish side that controlled both the ball and the rhythm of the evening.
“They defended extremely well,” Deschamps admitted afterwards. “They left us very little space. Because we made technical mistakes, it became difficult to create problems for them.”
Luis de la Fuente’s side has reached the final having conceded just one goal in seven matches, becoming the first team in World Cup history to record six clean-sheet victories in a single tournament.
It has now gone 37 matches unbeaten in all competitions and stands one win away from becoming only the third reigning European champion to follow it up with the World Cup.
Spain reduced Les Bleus to just 0.30 expected goals — France’s lowest attacking output at a World Cup in six decades. France attempted only 10 shots, with just one coming from inside 13 metres of the Spanish goal.
Spain’s defending begins long before opponents reach its centre-backs. Rodri and Ruiz monopolise possession, the press forces hurried decisions, passing lanes disappear and attacks are extinguished before they gather momentum.
“We couldn’t find solutions,” Deschamps admitted. “Compared with them, in our passing combinations and sequences, they’re also excellent at reading the game and intercepting passes. That’s normally one of our strengths. There was also a lot of merit on the opponent’s side.”
That collective understanding has become Spain’s greatest strength.
Lamine Yamal dazzles, Rodri dictates, Cubarsi defends with the assurance of a veteran, while Mikel Oyarzabal continues his habit of deciding the biggest matches.
Pedro Porro, whose first responsibility is usually to stop elite forwards, scored the second goal that put this contest to bed. The stars change from game to game because the system remains.
Spain has the most forced turnovers (303) in the tournament, has tried the most passes (4592) with a 91 percent success rate and also had the most defensive line breaks attempted (175) with a 65 percent success rate, while topping the charts in almost all movement metrics.
“We started almost four years ago with an idea, and we’ve been faithful to that idea and it’s brought us here,” De la Fuente said.
“Today we faced one of the best national teams in the world, but in front of them they had the best team in the world. That is the difference. These players deserve everything. Day after day they’ve shown their commitment, solidarity, generosity and talent. They make the difficult look easy.”
For years, Spain was admired for its possession but questioned for lacking the cutting edge.
This generation has found the balance. It monopolises the ball without becoming sterile, attacks without losing structure and defends without retreating.
Only one team has found a way past Unai Simon in seven World Cup matches.
With one game remaining, that may be the statistic that defines Spain’s tournament.

title: Watch: Messi's reaction after Argentina players find England goalkeeper's secret penalty notes
content:
Argentina's dramatic 2-1 victory over England in the 2026 FIFA World Cup semi-final produced another memorable moment after the final whistle, as
Lionel Messi and several teammates were filmed inspecting Jordan Pickford's water bottle, which reportedly contained detailed notes on Argentina's penalty takers.
The footage quickly went viral across social media after Argentina secured their place in Sunday's World Cup final against Spain.
Argentina narrowly avoided a penalty shootout
England looked set to reach the final after Anthony Gordon gave Thomas Tuchel's side the lead in the 55th minute.
But Argentina mounted another late comeback. Enzo Fernández levelled the match in the 85th minute before Lautaro Martínez headed home Messi's cross deep into stoppage time to seal a dramatic 2-1 victory, ensuring the reigning world champions progressed without requiring extra time or penalties.
That late winner also meant Pickford never had the opportunity to use the notes reportedly attached to his bottle.
Pickford's bottle draws Argentina's attention
Pickford has long been known for preparing detailed penalty cheat sheets during major tournaments, with notes taped to his water bottle to help identify opponents' preferred spot-kick placement.
Following the final whistle, television cameras caught several Argentina players gathering around the England goalkeeper's bottle, reportedly reading the handwritten notes that had been prepared in anticipation of a possible shootout.
Messi was seen grinning, and shaking his head alongside Enzo Fernández and other teammates as they examined the bottle, with the group appearing amused that the carefully prepared plans ultimately went unused.
While it has been widely reported that the notes mapped out Argentina's penalty takers, the exact contents of the bottle have not been officially confirmed.
The incident also highlighted how finely balanced the contest had become during the closing stages.
With the match level late on, Argentina's players reportedly took an interest in Pickford's preparations as the possibility of penalties loomed. Instead, Lautaro Martínez's stoppage-time winner rendered any shootout unnecessary.
hearted exchange with Pickford's bottle provided a fitting conclusion to a dramatic semi-final, with Messi's smile reflecting both relief and satisfaction after Argentina avoided what could have been another tense penalty decider.
Instead of facing one of football's most accomplished penalty-saving goalkeepers from 12 yards, Argentina completed the job in normal time and will now turn their attention to a blockbuster World Cup final against Spain.

title: 'Leo is still performing': Harry Kane uses Messi example to reveal his England World Cup future
content:
Harry Kane says it is far too early to decide whether the 2026
FIFA World Cup will be his last after England's hopes of reaching the final were ended by Argentina in a dramatic 2-1 semi-final defeat. The England captain admitted the latest setback felt painfully familiar after another deep tournament run ended one step short of the showpiece, but insisted he is not ready to place any limits on his international future.
Kane reflects on another near miss
England looked on course for a place in the World Cup final after Anthony Gordon fired Thomas Tuchel's side into the lead in the 55th minute in Atlanta.
However, the defending champions gradually seized control of the contest. Enzo Fernández equalised in the 85th minute before Lautaro Martínez headed home
Lionel Messi's cross deep into stoppage time to complete Argentina's comeback and book a final against Spain.
Reflecting on the defeat, Kane admitted England once again struggled to manage the closing stages of a major knockout match.
"It's a similar story to what's happened in previous tournaments I feel like," Kane said.
"We managed the momentum of the game so well for 60 minutes, we scored and got ahead.
"And then for one reason or another, we struggled to be on the ball, we struggled to put pressure on the ball and it allowed them to create more momentum and more attacks in our final third."
England's Harry Kane (9) and England's Jude Bellingham (10) are dejected at the end of the World Cup semifinal soccer match between England and Argentina in Atlanta, Wednesday, July 15, 2026. (AP Photo/Jeff Roberson)
goal advantage for more than 20 minutes against the reigning world champions proved too difficult.
"It's a normal mindset to try to hang on and hold on to the one-goal lead, but we're still 20 minutes out. We'll have to watch the game back and find out exactly how we can improve in those situations, but it's been probably the missing piece now for the last four tournaments."
England's attention turns to the third-place playoff
The defeat means England's wait for a first World Cup title since 1966 continues, although Tuchel's side still has one match remaining.
place playoff after Didier Deschamps' side were beaten 2-0 by Spain in the other semi-final.
Kane has once again led England deep into a major tournament, scoring six goals during the 2026 World Cup to finish among the competition's leading scorers while captaining his country to another last-four appearance.
Kane not ready to make retirement decision
Attention quickly shifted to whether the 32-year-old has played his final World Cup, with Kane set to be 36 by the time the next tournament arrives in 2030.
The Bayern Munich striker, however, made it clear that no decision will be made in the immediate aftermath of such a painful defeat, while also drawing inspiration from Lionel Messi's longevity at the highest level.
"Again, it's too early to talk about that," Kane told BBC Sport.
"I'm taking it year by year and the national team is my pride and joy. It's what I love to do more than anything.
"Obviously four years is a long way away. I'll be 33 this summer, but as you see on the other hand with Leo there, he's still performing at the highest level.
"I never want to put a limit on these things. I'll address situations as they come, but for now it's just about processing a tough loss."

title: FIFA WC 2026: Lionel Messi to join Cafu as only players to take the field in three World Cup finals
content:
When Lionel Messi takes to the field at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey for Argentina's World Cup final against Spain, he will become only the second player in history to actually play in three World Cup finals, joining Brazil's legendary right back Cafu in an exclusive club that has stood untouched for over two decades.
The distinction matters. Pelé, Matthäus and Ronaldo have all appeared in three World Cup final squads but each of them was an unused substitute in one of those three finals. Pelé was on the bench for Brazil's 1966 defeat to England. Matthäus was an unused substitute in Germany's 1986 final loss to Argentina. Ronaldo famously did not start the 1998 final against France, appearing only as a starter after a late and controversial reinstatement to the lineup following a reported seizure, though he was largely ineffective. None of them took the field in all three as active, participating players.
Cafu did. The Brazilian right back played every minute of the 1994, 1998 and 2002 World Cup finals, lifting the trophy in 1994 and 2002, and finishing as a runner-up in 1998. He remains the gold standard of World Cup final appearances.
Messi's three finals tell their own remarkable story. In 2014, he played every minute of Argentina's heartbreaking extra-time defeat to Germany in Brazil, winning the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player despite losing the final.
In 2022, he delivered one of the greatest individual performances in final history, scoring twice, including a stunning volley, before converting the decisive penalty in the shootout against France.
Now comes 2026. A third final. Against Spain. At 39 years old.
Cafu was 32 when he played his third final in 2002 and lifted the trophy. Messi is 39 and chasing the same ending. Should Argentina win on Sunday, Messi will not just have matched Cafu's record of playing in three finals. He will have surpassed it in the most meaningful way possible with two World Cup winners' medals instead of one.

title: Lionel Messi scripts World Cup history in England semi-final triumph with record no player has ever matched
content:
Lionel Messi once again proved why football's biggest moments continue to belong to him. The Argentina captain inspired the reigning champions to a dramatic 2-1 comeback victory over England in the 2026 FIFA World Cup semi-final, producing two decisive assists in the closing minutes to send La Albiceleste into a second consecutive World Cup final. In doing so, the 39-year-old shattered another long-standing World Cup record, becoming the outright leader for career assists at the tournament while also producing a statistical performance that no player has managed since Opta began recording detailed World Cup data in 1966.
final on its head as Argentina fight back
final in Atlanta developed into one of the most tactical and physically demanding matches of the tournament.
time as Argentina and England cancelled each other out through an intense midfield battle that produced 19 fouls before the interval. According to broadcasters, it was the first World Cup match since 1966 without a single shot recorded during the opening 30 minutes.
England finally broke the deadlock in the 55th minute. Harry Kane launched a swift counterattack before Morgan Rogers delivered a precise cross for Anthony Gordon, who calmly converted his first World Cup goal to hand the Three Lions a 1-0 advantage.
Argentina immediately increased the pressure.
Alexis Mac Allister struck the post with a diving header, while Jordan Pickford produced an outstanding save to deny Nico González as England desperately tried to preserve their lead.
The breakthrough eventually arrived five minutes from time. After Pickford had pushed away an initial effort, Messi quickly recycled possession and found Enzo Fernández on the edge of the area. The Chelsea midfielder curled a superb left-footed strike beyond the England goalkeeper in the 85th minute to restore parity.
Argentina's Lionel Messi celebrates after teammate Enzo Fernandez scored their side's first goal during the World Cup semifinal soccer match between England and Argentina in Atlanta, Wednesday, July 15, 2026. (AP Photo/Rebecca Blackwell)
With extra time looming, Messi delivered the decisive moment once again.
Mac Allister's header crashed against the post before Messi reacted first to collect the loose ball. The captain immediately whipped a precise cross into the six-yard box where Lautaro Martínez rose highest to head home the winning goal in stoppage time, completing another remarkable Argentine comeback and securing a place in Sunday's World Cup final against Spain.
Argentina's Lautaro Martinez hugs teammate Lionel Messi celebrating after scoring their side's second goal during the World Cup semifinal soccer match between England and Argentina in Atlanta, Wednesday, July 15, 2026. (AP Photo/Rebecca Blackwell)
Messi breaks World Cup assist record
The two assists carried Messi into territory no player has previously reached.
According to Opta, the Argentina captain now has 12 assists across his World Cup career, the highest total recorded since detailed tournament data began in 1966.
- Lionel Messi (Argentina) — 12 assists in 33 World Cup matches
- Pelé (Brazil) — 10 assists in 14 matches
- Fritz Walter (West Germany) — 9 assists in 11 matches
- Raymond Kopa (France) — 8 assists in 8 matches
- Diego Maradona (Argentina) — 8 assists in 21 matches
- Uwe Seeler (West Germany) — 8 assists in 21 matches
Another record belongs to Messi
Messi's all-round display was as historic as it was decisive. Official Opta data credited him with nine successful dribbles alongside two assists against England.
That made him the first player on record since 1966 to complete at least nine successful dribbles and provide two assists in the same World Cup knockout match.
He also stretched his run of scoring or assisting to 13 consecutive World Cup matches, another record unmatched since Opta's detailed database began in 1966.
Messi's latest masterpiece also strengthened his individual tournament numbers.
He now has eight goals and four assists at the 2026 World Cup, moving him to the top of the Golden Boot standings. Although he is level with Kylian Mbappé on eight goals, Messi's four assists surpass Mbappé's three, giving the Argentina captain the tiebreak advantage with the France forward now out of the tournament
The victory sends Lionel Scaloni's side into a second consecutive World Cup final, where they will meet an unbeaten Spain team seeking back-to-back major international titles.
For Messi, however, the night in Atlanta represented something even greater than another appearance in football's biggest match.
Every major World Cup record once considered untouchable continues to fall.
Against England, he became the tournament's greatest creator, produced a statistical performance never previously recorded in a knockout match, and once again demonstrated that, even at 39 years of age, Argentina's pursuit of another world title still revolves around the brilliance of their captain.

title: FIFA WC 2026: Euro holders Spain to battle Copa America champions Argentina in historic World Cup final
content:
The stage is officially set for the ultimate footballing spectacle. Following Argentina’s comeback semifinal victory over England on Wednesday night, the FIFA World Cup 2026 grand finale will feature a dream showdown that scripts an unprecedented chapter in football history.
When powerhouse Spain faces reigning global champions Argentina at the MetLife Stadium on Sunday, it will mark the first time a World Cup final takes place between the reigning UEFA European champions and the reigning CONMEBOL Copa América champions. It is a mouth-watering winner-takes-all clash that will decisively crown the absolute rulers of the global game.
Benz Stadium. Orchestrated once again by the ageless brilliance of
Lionel Messi, the Albiceleste managed to successfully nullify the athletic threat of Jude Bellingham and Harry Kane and beat the Three Lions 2-1 with goals from Enzo Fernandez and Lautaro Martinez. The tactical triumph keeps Argentina's dream of securing back-to-back world titles alive, setting up a blockbusting encounter against a soaring Spanish side that easily swept past France 2-0 a night earlier.
The historical weight of this fixture cannot be overstated. While cross-continental rivalries have defined the tournament's history, never before have the simultaneous masters of Europe and South America collided directly with the World Cup trophy on the line.
Generation vs. GreatnessBeyond tactical structures, the final serves as a poetic passing of the guard. The match features a direct generational battlefield between Spain's teenage phenom Lamine Yamal, who completely tore up the French defense just days after his 19th birthday and the 39-year-old Messi, who is arguably on his final mission to solidify his status as the undisputed greatest of all time.
Spain’s elegant possession football will go toe-to-toe with Argentina's fierce grit and tournament intuition. With both heavyweights operating at the absolute peak of their continental powers, the sporting world is guaranteed an unforgettable battle for immortality in East Rutherford.

title: ARG Vs ENG, SF, Highlights: 2-1; Messi and Co complete wild turnaround to reach World Cup final
content:
England captain Harry Kane was left devastated after another painful World Cup exit, admitting the team gave everything but could not hold on against Argentina.
“Gutted for the boys, gutted for everyone – the team, the staff, the fans,” Kane said, speaking to BBC On, eafter the 2-1 semi-final defeat.
England had taken the lead and controlled large parts of the game, but Kane admitted they struggled to maintain their intensity after going ahead.
“When we went 1-0 up we seemed to try and hold on, which at this level is not enough,” he said.
Kane praised the effort from his teammates, saying: “The lads have given every last bit of running, blood, sweat, tears.”
Reflecting on Argentina’s comeback, he added that England were unable to deal with the pressure once the momentum shifted.
“After the goal… it was just wave after wave. Lads were putting blocks in but, in the end, it just wasn’t enough.”
Heading into the grand finale, Argentina have scored 19 goals and conceded 7 across their seven matches at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
With their dramatic 2-1 victory over England in the semi-final, Argentina have reached their seventh World Cup final in history. The achievement moves them clear of Italy and level with Brazil for the joint-second most final appearances in tournament history, behind Germany.
It is also Argentina’s second consecutive World Cup final appearance, following their run to the title match in 2022. The defending champions now have the chance to lift the trophy in back-to-back tournaments.
They will face Spain on Sunday, July 19, at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey, with the 2026 FIFA World Cup crown on the line.
Argentina are heading back to the World Cup final.
The moment the final whistle went, several Argentina players collapsed to the ground after giving everything in a brutal, exhausting semi-final. It was a match that demanded every ounce of energy, and the holders found a way when it mattered most.
The celebrations quickly moved towards the stands as the Argentina squad joined their supporters. The blue and white army inside the stadium erupted as Messi and Co completed another unforgettable World Cup night.
For England, it is another painful ending. They controlled large parts of the game, took the lead through Anthony Gordon and looked on course for the final, but Argentina’s late fightback changed everything.
Argentina will now face Spain in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final on Sunday, July 19 at MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford, New Jersey.
England’s tournament continues with the third-place play-off against France.
TIME: Argentina are in the World Cup final!
Lionel Messi and Argentina have done it again. In a semi-final that had everything, the holders produced another unbelievable comeback to turn a 1-0 deficit into a 2-1 victory over England in Atlanta.
England looked on course for the final after Anthony Gordon’s second-half opener, but Argentina refused to fold. Enzo Fernández’s stunning strike brought them level before Lautaro Martínez’s late header completed the turnaround in stoppage time.
Messi once again delivered the defining moments, creating chances, controlling the chaos and helping Argentina find a way through when the pressure was at its highest.
England fought until the final whistle, but Argentina’s experience, resilience and big-game mentality proved decisive.
The reigning champions are back in the final. One more game stands between Messi and another World Cup crown.
Desperate times call for desperate measures.
final. Marcus Rashford and Ivan Toney are introduced for John Stones and Djed Spence, with Dan Burn now pushed up alongside Toney as a centre-forward for the final minutes.
Argentina have done a superb job managing the game since taking the lead, but England have left themselves with a mountain to climb. The Three Lions have barely been able to get the ball moving after Lautaro Martínez’s late header, and now they are relying on direct play and aerial pressure.
There are only a few minutes left for England to keep their World Cup dream alive.
final on its head.
Just moments after Alexis Mac Allister rattles the post with a low drive, Argentina keep the pressure alive. The move begins with a moment of brilliance from Lionel Messi, who delivers a long, curling pass from the right flank to keep the attack moving.
The ball is worked back into the danger area, the cross comes in from the right, and Lautaro Martínez is there at close range to meet it with a powerful header.
England are stunned. They had the lead for most of the second half, but Argentina have completed the comeback late in Atlanta.
Lautaro may have just sent Argentina into the World Cup final.
The drama continues in Atlanta.
John Stones briefly stops play after appearing to pick up a neck issue, with the England defender receiving treatment from the physio. After a few tense moments, he is cleared to continue.
The fourth official signals nine minutes of added time, meaning there is still plenty of football left in this semi-final.
Play resumes with Argentina immediately back on the front foot, pushing forward as they look to complete the comeback after Enzo Fernández’s stunning equaliser brought them level. England are hanging on, but one counter could still decide everything.
Argentina have their equaliser, and what a way to get it!
The corner is taken short, and Lionel Messi makes something happen once again. The captain drops his shoulder on the right flank, just outside the penalty area, and picks out Enzo Fernández in space.
Fernández takes one touch before unleashing a stunning strike from around 20 yards. Jordan Pickford dives across, but there is simply too much power and precision as the ball flies into the side netting.
England had been inviting pressure for too long, and Argentina finally make them pay. Messi provides the moment of magic, Fernández delivers the finish, and this World Cup semi-final is level again in Atlanta.
Both sides are going for it in the final stretch.
Argentina make another attacking move as Lautaro Martínez replaces Nicolás Tagliafico, with Lionel Scaloni putting more firepower on the pitch in search of an equaliser.
England respond with a double change of their own. Dan Burn and O’Reilly come on for Declan Rice and Reece James as Thomas Tuchel looks to add fresh energy and defensive strength.
The pressure has been relentless from Argentina. A barrage of crosses causes chaos in the England box, with James ending up down after the latest wave of pressure. There are bodies flying everywhere as England try to hold onto their one-goal lead.
Argentina are turning the pressure up.
Lionel Messi produces another moment of quality, lifting an effortless chip over the top of the England defence for Nicolás González to chase. The forward gets his head to it, but sends the effort well wide. The flag eventually goes up for offside, although it was extremely tight and would have been a huge moment had it found the net.
Moments earlier, another brilliant Rodrigo De Paul delivery almost causes problems, with Alexis Mac Allister heading straight at Jordan Pickford.
England are sitting deeper and deeper, taking the risk of defending their narrow lead as Argentina keep pushing for the equaliser.
England are living dangerously.
Argentina's pressure finally produces a golden chance as Rodrigo De Paul, just moments after coming on, delivers a superb curling cross into a packed penalty area.
Alexis Mac Allister finds a pocket of space and throws himself at the ball with a powerful header, but his effort crashes back off the far post. Jordan Pickford is beaten, and England can only watch as the woodwork comes to their rescue.
Mac Allister did brilliantly to escape his marker, but with the goal at his mercy, he knows he should have done better.
Argentina are turning the screw, and England's one-goal advantage is looking increasingly fragile.
half restart brings a flurry of changes from both benches.
final. Rodrigo De Paul, Gonzalo Montiel and Nicolás Otamendi enter the action, replacing Giuliano Simeone, Nahuel Molina and Lisandro Martínez.
Thomas Tuchel responds with a change of his own, with Ezri Konsa coming on for goalscorer Anthony Gordon as England switch their focus towards protecting their lead.
Argentina have dominated possession since falling behind, but England remain organised and dangerous on the counter. The next 20 minutes could decide who books a place in the World Cup final.
Jordan Pickford keeps England in front with a massive save.
perfect delivery from Lionel Messi, who floats a dangerous ball into the penalty area. Nicolás González meets it first time and looks certain to score, but Pickford reacts brilliantly, diving low to his right to push the effort away.
It's a huge moment in the semi-final. Argentina thought they had their equaliser, but England's goalkeeper produces one of the saves of the tournament to preserve the Three Lions' slender advantage.
England finally break out of their own half with Morgan Rogers leading a dangerous counter down the right.
With plenty of white shirts arriving in the box, Rogers rolls the ball back perfectly for Declan Rice on the edge of the area. The midfielder looks to whip a teasing delivery across goal, but completely miscues it, sending a tame ball straight into the grateful arms of Emiliano Martínez.
It was a huge opportunity to put Argentina under real pressure, but England fail to make the most of one of their few attacking moments since taking the lead.
Argentina make the first substitution of the evening as Nicolás González replaces Leandro Paredes.
The winger is immediately involved, meeting a deep cross from the right and cleverly trying to nod the ball back across the face of goal. For a moment, England's defence is stretched, but the white shirts react quickly to hack the danger away before an Argentina forward can pounce.
England are under sustained pressure.
Since Gordon's opener, Argentina have almost completely taken control of possession, pinning England deep inside their own half. At times, England have all ten outfield players packed around the edge of their own penalty area, content to absorb wave after wave of attacks before looking to break on the counter.
The latest effort comes from Enzo Fernández, who decides to have another crack from long range, but his ambitious strike sails comfortably over the crossbar.
England have barely had a touch since taking the lead, but for now, they are standing firm as Argentina continue to search for a way back into the semi-final.
Argentina are throwing everything at England now.
After England clear the initial corner, the holders keep the attack alive with wave after wave of pressure. The ball eventually finds Lionel Messi inside the penalty area, and the Argentine captain instantly has England's defenders scrambling.
Messi slaloms through a crowd of white shirts, twisting and turning in trademark fashion as he searches for an opening, but England refuse to bite. Bodies get in the way, the defence stands firm, and the danger is eventually cleared.
The tempo has gone through the roof since Gordon's opener, with Argentina pushing relentlessly for an equaliser.
That could prove just as valuable as a goal.
Giuliano Simeone looks clean through inside the penalty area after getting the wrong side of Djed Spence, but the England full-back somehow recovers with a perfectly timed sliding challenge, poking the ball away with the outside of his right boot.
It is a breathtaking piece of defending. A split-second later and it could easily have been a penalty, or worse, an equaliser for Argentina.
Spence leaps to his feet and celebrates the tackle with as much passion as if he had found the back of the net, with his England team-mates rushing over to congratulate him on a potentially match-saving intervention.
England strike with a brilliantly timed counterattack.
The move begins after Declan Rice collects a loose clearance and quickly flicks the ball wide to Morgan Rogers on the right. Rogers wastes no time, curling a wicked cross towards the far post before Argentina can recover their shape.
The timing of the delivery is perfect. Anthony Gordon gets the wrong side of Nahuel Molina, keeps his eyes on the bounce and guides a composed finish past Emiliano Martínez from close range.
It's a superbly worked goal and a huge moment in the semi-final. Tuchel's decision to start Rogers has paid off in spectacular fashion, with the winger producing the decisive cross to give England a priceless 1-0 lead.
England have the breakthrough, and it's Anthony Gordon who delivers.
half pressure into a goal as Gordon arrives at the right moment to fire England ahead, sending the travelling supporters into absolute delirium.
to-end opening to the second, England strike first in Atlanta. Argentina are stunned, while Thomas Tuchel's side are now just over half an hour away from a place in the FIFA World Cup 2026 final.
Cristian Romero has no complaints this time.
As Jude Bellingham bursts away on another England counterattack, the Argentine defender simply wraps his arms around him and drags him back, ending the move before it can gather pace. It is more wrestling than defending, and the referee immediately reaches for his pocket.
Romero becomes Argentina's second booked centre-back, joining Lisandro Martínez in the book. With both defenders now on yellow cards, they'll have to tread carefully for the remainder of this tense semi-final.
As expected, Lionel Messi steps up to take Argentina's corner, but instead of swinging it into the box, he opts for a short routine. England read it well and quickly snuff out the move before it can develop.
The Three Lions immediately spring forward in transition. They race up the pitch and whip in two dangerous low crosses from either flank, but neither delivery finds an England shirt inside the area.
The game has suddenly burst into life, with both sides attacking at pace after a cagey first half.
Argentina have come out with far more purpose after the restart.
The holders keep the ball patiently inside England's half before working an opening for Julián Álvarez, who bursts clear after getting the better of Djed Spence. The striker rifles a powerful effort towards the near post, but Jordan Pickford reacts sharply to make an excellent save.
Álvarez pounces on the loose ball and has another go moments later, but this time he can only find the side netting. It's Argentina's most dangerous spell of the match so far.
We're back under way in Atlanta.
England eventually get the second half started, although the opening kick has to be retaken after the referee pulls play back moments after the whistle.
There are no changes from either manager at the break, with both Thomas Tuchel and Lionel Scaloni sticking with the XIs that finished the opening half as England restart proceedings.
The players are back out for the second half.
England emerge from the tunnel first, with Argentina following moments later as the atmosphere inside the stadium cranks up another notch. There are still a few words being exchanged with the referee before play resumes, a reminder of how feisty the opening 45 minutes were.
The countdown to the restart begins, and England will get the second half under way as both sides look to find the breakthrough in a finely balanced World Cup semi-final.
If you were keeping score another way, it would be England 7–12 Argentina — that's the foul count at half-time.
final has been packed with crunching tackles, shirt pulls and heated confrontations, but short on clear-cut chances. The closest either side came to breaking the deadlock was Enzo Fernández's superb curling effort from 25 yards that drifted just over the crossbar with Jordan Pickford scrambling across his goal.
It's been a bruising, hard-fought first half, but the biggest battle so far has been for control rather than the scoreboard. Everything is still on the line heading into the second half.
The referee brings an absorbing first half to a close with the score still locked at 0-0.
England were the brighter side for much of the opening 30 minutes, controlling possession and repeatedly pushing Argentina back. John Stones squandered their best chance when he glanced Declan Rice's inviting free-kick into the side netting, while Reece James caused problems with dangerous deliveries from the right.
Argentina gradually grew into the contest, with Lionel Messi beginning to find more space between the lines. Enzo Fernández came closest for La Albiceleste with a superb curling effort that flew inches over before Messi started orchestrating attacks in the closing stages.
The game has been every bit as physical as expected. Crunching tackles, shirt pulls and constant arguments surrounded almost every stoppage, with Elliot Anderson and Lisandro Martínez both entering the referee's notebook.
It has been a gripping, hard-fought semi-final so far, but despite the intensity, neither goalkeeper has been seriously tested. Everything remains to play for after the break.
The fourth official signals three minutes of added time at the end of the first half.
After England controlled much of the opening 30 minutes, the momentum has shifted. Argentina are beginning to enjoy longer spells of possession and are pushing further into England's half, asking more questions of the Three Lions' defence.
England, meanwhile, look like a side that could do with the half-time whistle to regroup before the break.
England finally break out after Argentina's longest spell of possession, but Morgan Rogers' run is cut short by Lisandro Martínez.
The Argentine defender grabs a handful of Rogers' shirt and keeps pulling as the England winger tries to burst away, leaving the referee with an easy decision. Martínez is shown Argentina's first yellow card of the evening.
The home crowd responds with ironic cheers as England's counterattack is stopped in its tracks, with Martínez joining Elliot Anderson in the referee's notebook.
Argentina nearly produce the breakthrough.

The shot whistles just over the crossbar with Jordan Pickford at full stretch. The England goalkeeper throws himself across goal, but had it dipped a fraction lower, he would have had no chance. Argentina's best opportunity of the first half so far.
It finally boils over.
Lionel Messi wriggles away from a string of England challenges before Elliot Anderson brings him down with a heavy tackle. The referee has no hesitation this time, producing the first yellow card of the match.
Argentina's players immediately surround the official, demanding stronger action as tempers flare once again. After several robust challenges in the opening half, the referee finally reaches for his pocket, with Anderson becoming the first player booked in this fiery World Cup semi-final.
England continue to pile on the pressure.
After another slick attacking move, Reece James wins a free-kick on the left edge of the penalty area. He swings a dangerous ball into the box, forcing Emiliano Martínez to punch clear under pressure.
The loose ball refuses to settle as both sides battle for it in a frantic spell of head tennis inside the area, but Argentina eventually survive the scramble and hack it clear before England can get a clean effort away.
England come inches away.
Declan Rice curls an inviting delivery towards the back post where John Stones gets across his marker. The defender tries to guide his header back across goal but only manages to send it wide into the side netting.
Stones immediately puts his hands on his head in frustration. It was England's clearest opening of the match, and the centre-back knows he should have tested Emiliano Martínez from there.
A moment of brilliance from Jude Bellingham finally sparks England.
The midfielder shrugs off one challenge, powers through the middle and is eventually chopped down by Enzo Fernández around 25 yards from goal. It is a cynical foul and hands England a free-kick in a very dangerous position.
Fernández is walking a fine line here. After his heavy early challenge on Elliot Anderson, another late foul leaves him fortunate not to be in the referee's notebook. England now have a real chance to make Argentina pay.

Argentina quickly regain possession inside England's half and work it to Lionel Messi, but the Argentine captain cannot get the ball under control. His first touch lets him down and it rolls harmlessly out of play, giving England a goal-kick.
The referee pauses play for the scheduled hydration break.
Both teams head over to the touchline for a quick drink and instructions from the coaching staff after an intense opening 25 minutes. England have seen more of the ball, while Argentina have gradually grown into the contest after a quiet start.
Jude Bellingham is penalised after mistiming a challenge on Alexis Mac Allister, handing Argentina a free-kick around 35 yards from goal.
Rather than swinging the ball into the box, Argentina play the free-kick short. The move works its way into the area before an England defender gets a touch, with the ball going behind for Argentina's first corner of the match.
England come close to opening Argentina up.
A slick passing move sees Morgan Rogers release Reece James on the overlap down the right. The England full-back bursts into the edge of the box and drills a dangerous low cross across the face of goal.
The delivery has plenty of pace, but it is just close enough for Emiliano Martínez, who reacts quickly and dives forward to gather before any England attacker can get a touch.
Argentina launch their first meaningful attack of the match after a long ball is worked out to the right flank.
The holders push into England's half, but there is little support arriving in the box. The resulting cross from deep sails beyond everyone, allowing England's defence to clear without any trouble.
After spending much of the opening 15 minutes on the back foot, Argentina finally show signs of life in attack, but England remain largely in control.
The temperature is rising in Atlanta.
Another physical coming together brings play to a halt, with Leandro Paredes called over by the referee for a stern talking-to despite Argentina retaining possession for the restart.
On the touchline, Thomas Tuchel is furious, repeatedly gesturing in frustration as England's bench voices its displeasure. The challenges are flying in from both sides, and the referee already has his hands full trying to keep this World Cup semi-final under control.
England have made the brighter start in Atlanta, keeping Argentina pinned inside their own half for much of the opening exchanges.
Declan Rice's corner was comfortably headed clear by Cristian Romero, but the Three Lions quickly regained possession and continued to dictate the tempo. Argentina, meanwhile, have been happy to break up play with a series of robust challenges whenever England threaten to build momentum.
The holders are yet to settle on the ball, while England continue to ask the early questions.
England have the first corner of the match after a lively move down the right forces Argentina to concede.

La Albiceleste eventually deal with the delivery, but England are beginning to apply some early pressure.

Anderson has already been at the centre of a few physical challenges after his early foul on Lionel Messi, and Argentina's players are making sure the Newcastle midfielder knows they have not forgotten that opening tackle.

England, in white shirts, blue shorts and blue socks, get the second World Cup semi-final underway against Argentina, who are wearing their all-black away kit.
Ninety minutes separate these two footballing giants from a place in the FIFA World Cup 2026 final.
